did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes a hybrid deep learning (HDL) approach for skin cancer diagnosis. It uses 3D wavelet transform for feature extraction from dermoscopic images and employs a deep learning classifier with softmax activation to perform multiclass classification (normal, benign, malignant) of skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original experimental study proposing a hybrid deep learning (HDL) approach for skin cancer diagnosis. It details specific methodology including 3D wavelet transform and subband fusion, describes the implementation of a multiclass classification system, and reports original performance results (99.33% accuracy) on the PH2 database. While it includes a related works section, the primary contribution is the development and validation of a new technical method, not a summary of prior literature.

title: A hybrid deep learning approach for skin cancer diagnosis using subband fusion of 3D wavelets
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid deep learning (HDL) approach for automated skin cancer diagnosis using dermoscopic images to address the subjectivity of clinical routines. The method employs simple median filtering for preprocessing, followed by feature extraction via subband fusion of 3D wavelet transforms. Multiclass classification into normal, benign, and malignant categories is performed using the HDL classifier with softmax activation. The system was evaluated on the PH2 database, utilizing accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity as performance metrics. The proposed HDL approach achieved an average accuracy of 99.33%, with sensitivity and specificity exceeding 90%, outperforming conventional classifiers and other deep learning methods. These results indicate that the HDL approach offers a robust and balanced solution for the early diagnosis of melanoma and other skin cancers.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three classes in the multiclass classification task on the PH2 database. The system discriminates normal skin images from benign and malignant ones.
One of the three classes in the multiclass classification task on the PH2 database. Includes benign skin lesions as distinct from malignant ones.

One of the three classes in the multiclass classification task on the PH2 database. The paper notes that melanoma is the deadliest skin cancer and the proposed approach has applications in its early diagnosis.

Mentioned in the introduction as the deadliest skin cancer and in the conclusion as a specific application for the proposed HDL approach. However, the experimental classification on the PH2 database uses the broader 'malignant' category rather than distinguishing melanoma from other malignancies.
Mentioned in the introduction as a type of skin cancer, but not used as a specific label in the PH2 database experiments.
Mentioned in the introduction as a type of skin cancer, but not used as a specific label in the PH2 database experiments.

all_task_types.target: Multiclass classification of skin lesions into normal, benign, and malignant categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states that the system performs 'multiclass classification' using a hybrid deep learning (HDL) approach with softmax activation. The target classes are 'normal', 'benign', and 'malignant' skin images from the PH2 database. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity, which are standard metrics for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: PH2 database
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic image database for research and benchmarking containing 200 dermoscopic color images (RGB) with melanocytic lesions. Each image size is 768 × 560 pixels.

use.training: Used for training the hybrid deep learning (HDL) classifier. The paper does not specify the exact split ratio for training, validation, and testing, but states the system was analyzed using this database.
use.testing: Used for testing the performance of the HDL approach. Performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) were calculated on the images from this database.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To divide the PH2 database images into training and testing sets for evaluating the HDL approach.
To combine outputs from VGG models in the HDL classifier by assigning weights based on the accuracy of each model.
To visualize and analyze the performance of the HDL approach for various levels of 3D-DWT decomposition.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics on the PH2 database.

PH2 database.notes: The HDL approach was evaluated on the PH2 database using a 50:50 random split for training and testing. The best performance was achieved with 3rd-level 3D-DWT subband fusion. The system classifies images into normal, benign, and malignant categories. For the optimal HDL model, 97% of normal and benign cases and 98% of malignant cases were classified correctly.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid Deep Learning (HDL) approach using subband fusion of 3D wavelets

components: 1-Block model: Single convolution layer with 32 filters + max pooling layer
2-Block model: 1-Block model + single convolution layer with 64 filters + max pooling layer
3-Block model: 2-Block model + single convolution layer with 128 filters + max pooling layer
architecture_details.fusion_method: Subband fusion of 3D Discrete Wavelet Transform (3D-DWT) using Max Fusion Rule based on local energy of 3x3 window
architecture_details.ensemble_method: Weighted average method in the output layer, where weights are assigned based on the accuracy of each VGG model

classification_or_output_head.activation: Sigmoid (for individual VGG models) / Softmax (for final HDL output)
classification_or_output_head.decision_method: Weighted average of outputs from 1-VGG, 2-VGG, and 3-VGG models

preprocessing_techniques: Simple median filtering (nonlinear filter) with a window size of 21 × 21 to remove impulse and random noise and hair artifacts while preserving image details.
conclusions: The proposed hybrid deep learning (HDL) approach using subband fusion of 3D wavelets effectively discriminates normal, benign, and malignant skin images.
The HDL approach achieved an average accuracy of 99.33%, with sensitivity and specificity exceeding 90% on the PH2 database.
The optimal performance was obtained using the 3rd-level 3D-DWT subband fusion within the HDL architecture.
The HDL approach outperformed conventional classifiers (such as KNN, SVM, BoF) and other deep learning methods (such as NN ensemble, NSCT, Boltzmann entropy) in terms of classification accuracy.
The study confirms that the HDL approach provides a robust, noninvasive, and objective method for the early diagnosis of skin cancer, addressing the subjectivity of clinical routines.
The system classifies dermoscopic images into three categories: normal, benign, and malignant.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the PH2 database, which contains only 200 images (80 normal, 80 benign, 40 malignant), limiting the generalizability of the results due to the small sample size and lack of external validation.

The data splitting method is described only as a '50:50 random split' without specifying the exact number of training, validation, or testing images, raising concerns about reproducibility and potential data leakage if the split was not stratified or repeated.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of the performance improvements over baseline methods.

The 'malignant' class in the PH2 database is a heterogeneous group that includes melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma; the model does not distinguish between these specific malignant types, which limits its clinical utility for specific cancer subtypes.
Comparative analysis includes several baseline methods (e.g., KNN, SVM, BoF) where accuracy is not reported, making a complete performance comparison difficult.
The preprocessing step uses a large median filter window (21x21) to remove hair, which may inadvertently blur or remove fine lesion details critical for diagnosis.

The paper claims the system addresses the shortage of ophthalmologists, but the correct specialist for skin cancer diagnosis is a dermatologist, indicating a potential terminology error in the clinical context.
